My name is Silvano Corporeal, I am the director of Everyman, and the artistic director of Laurier Historical Theatre Society, which, if you didn't know, exists. So thank you all for joining us. This is our first, and possibly last, production of the magazine. First of all, I'd like to thank a few people. Most people are in the program, so you can see their names. Fear not, I shall speak for thee. Have mercy on me, God Almighty, and mother named Mary. Short our ends and finish our pain. Let us go, and never come again. Here I cry thee, mercy! In thy hands, my soul, I commend. Receive it, Lord, that it be not lost. To save me from the fiend's boast. That I may be with that blessed host that shall be saved the day of doom. In manus tuas of might most. Forever commendo spiritum Thank you. In manus tuas, of might most, forever commendo spiritum, ma'am. Now hath he suffered that which we all shall endure, The good deeds shall make all sure. Now hath he made ending, methinketh I hear angels sing, And make great joy and melody, where every man's soul shall receive to be. Now come, excellent, elect spouse to Jesu, your boss thou shalt build, now that thy soul has taken thy body from, thy reckoning is crystal clear, and thou shalt enter the heavenly sphere, unto which all ye that liveth well shalt go after the day of doom. This memory may all men have in mind, ye hearers, take it of worth, old and young. And for safe pride, for he deceiveth you in the end. And remember, beauty, five wits, strength, and discretion. They all at last do every man forsake. Save his good deeds, them there he doth take. But beware for, and may be small, before God he hath no help at all. There too help the Trinity. Amen, say ye, forsake charity. Sadie, for St. Cherry. Thank you. Applaus © BF-WATCH TV 2021

timestamps.text: Well, Facebook told us we were popular at the club, but we didn't realize we were this popular, so thank you all for joining us. My name is Silvano Corporeal, I am the director of Everyman, and the artistic director of Laurier Historical Theatre Society, which, if you didn't know, exists. So thank you all for joining us. This is our first, and possibly last, production of the magazine. First of all, I'd like to thank a few people. Most people are in the program, so you can see their names. You may know them, you may not. We owe all of them so, so much. There are a few people I really need to thank. Professor Alicia McKenzie over there for giving us this idea. She kind of slipped it into my mind several years ago, and it led me to this, so thank you very much. We also owe our support to Dr. Nyman, who is not here yet, but he was very supportive of this club and invited us to be part of Legal Day 2016, so thank you to him as well. Also, Katie Lamb did our makeup, and she is not in the program because she joined us last minute, so thank you very much. As well, Daniel Lance, who is in the program. He is our musical director, which has been very, very helpful. Nicole Moraira, back there, my partner in crime.
Couldn't have done this without you.
And of course, my good friend, Eric Biro,
for being every man and helping us out
in that regard.
So this, if you didn't know, this is a medieval morality
play.
It's super Christian, understandably.
But it has a meaning that transcends
the very universally Catholic nature of the Middle Ages.
Thank you. Christian, understandably, but it has a meaning that transcends the very universally Catholic
nature of the Middle Ages. It's a play that we can take meaning from even now, and I invite
you to look at it a little deeper when you watch it and see that meaning, see what is
in that that anyone can learn from. Yes, so thank you all very much, and please enjoy

the show. Svanskog Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavro Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros Stavros I pray you all give your audience and hear this matter with reverence, by figure a moral play. The summoning of every man called it is, that of our lives and endings shows how transitory we be all day. This matter is wonders precious, but the intent of it is more gracious and swift to bear away. The story saith, man, in the beginning, look well and take good heed to thy ending, be ye never so gay. Ye think sin in the beginning full sweet, which in the end causeth thy soul to weep, when the body lieth in clay. Here you shall see how fellowship and jolty, both strength, discretion, and beauty, will fade from me as a flower in May. You shall hear how our heaven king calleth every man to a general reckoning Give audience and hear what he doth say I perceive here in my majesty How that all creatures be to me unkind Living Living without dread in worldly prosperity, a ghostly sight that people be so blind. Drowned in sin, they knew me not for their God. In worldly riches is all their mind. They fear not my right wiseness, the sharp rod, my law that I showed when I for them died. They forget
clean and shedding of my blood red. I hanged between two, it cannot be denied. To get them
life, I suffered to be dead. I healed their feet with thorns hurt with my head. I could

do no more than I did truly, yet now I see the people do clean forsake me. They use the 7, 10, 10, 10, 10, 10, 10, 10, 10, 10, Now in the world we made commendable, and thus they leave of angels the heavenly company. Every man liveth so after his own pleasure, and yet of their life they be nothing sure. I see the more that I then forbear, the worse they be from year to year. All that liveth appeareth vast. Thereto I must in all the haste have a reckoning of every man's person. For, and I leave the people thus alone in their life and wicked tempests, verily they will become much worse than beasts, for one now would by envy another upheat. Charity they do all clean for death. I hope it well that in my glory every man should make his mansion. There too I have them all elect, yet now I see like treasures deject. They thank me not for the pleasures that I to them meant, nor yet for their being that I them have lent. I proffered the people great multitude of mercy, and few there be that asketh it heartily.

They be so cumbered with worldly riches. The needs on them I must do justice. On every man living without fear. Where art thou, death, thou mighty messenger? O mighty God, I am here at your will, your commandment to fulfill.

Go thou to every man, and show him in my name a pilgrimage he must on him take, which he in no wise may escape, and that ye bring with him a sure reckoning, without delay or any terror. Or, I will in the world go run over all And cruelly I shall outsearch both the great and the small He that liveth feastily out of God's laws And dreadeth not falling Every man that loveth riches I shall strike with dart. His sight were to blind, from heaven to depart.
I'll accept that I'll be his good friend.
Then in hell for to dwell, world without end.
And lo, yonder I see every man walking.
Little he think upon my coming.
His mind upon his fleshly lusts and treasure.

Great pain they shall cause him to endure before the Lord, Heaven's King. Every day, stand still, hither art thou going thus gaily, hast thou thy maker forgot? Why askest thou? What is't thou wit? Yea, yea, I shall show thee, I am sent to thee hastily, from God, of God's majesty. What, sent to me? Yea, certainly, though ye may have forgotten here, he thinketh upon thee in the heavenly sphere, and as here we depart, ye shall know. What desireth God of me? That I shall tell thee also, a reckoning he will needs have, without any leaguer respite. To give a reckoning longer leisure, I crave. This blind matter troubleth my wit. On thee thou must take a long journey. Therefore, proof, by book of accounts, take with thee. return again thou cannot find way, before God thou shalt answer, and show, thine many bad deeds, while at good, but a few, how thou hast ended thy life, and thou with lies, before the chief lord of paradise, how the do's have we were in that way, for with thou well, thou shalt make none at her name. Full unready I am, such reckoning to give. I know thee not. What messenger art thou? I am Death, and no man dreadeth. Every man rests, and no man spareth. For it is God's commandment that all to me should be obedient. O death, thou comest when I had thee least in mind. In thy power it lieth me to save thee. Yet of my good will I will give thee, if thou wilt be kind. Yea, a thousand pounds shalt thou have, and defer this matter till another day. Nay, nay, and be By no way. I set not by Gold, silver, and riches. Nay, pope, Emperor, king, Nay, princess, or At I would receive gifts Great of a world I might Get. But my Custom is clean, and contrary. Come thee hence, tarry. Alas, shall I have no respite? I may say death giveth no warning. To think on thee is to make my heart sick. For full unready is my book of reckoning. But for twelve years and I might have abided, my counting book I would make so clear. Wherefore, death, I pray thee for God's mercy, spare me till I be provided a remedy. The availeth not to cry, weep, or pray, but haste thee that thou wert gone this journey, and prove thy friends, who in deed thou can. For with thou wilt I abided no, and in this world each living creature, for Adam's sin must die, of nature. O gracious God, in the high seat celestial, have mercy on me in this most deed. Have I shall no manner of company from this veil terrestrial of my acquaint that way need to lead.
Yea, if any would be so hearty, some could go with thee and keep thee company. But, Hydee, thou wert gone this journey to give your reckoning before the Lord's magnificence. Oh, what? Oops.

What? What weenest thou thy life was given thee, and thy worldly goods as well? I had ween so verily. Nay, nay, t'was but lengthy, and after thou art gone, Another for a while shall have it, and then go thereereto, even as thou hast done. Every man thou art mad, thou hast thy wits by, and yet in this world shall not amend thy life, for suddenly I do come. O wretched Cain, whither shall I flee? Shall I have no respite? O gosh! Death, shall I not be saved?
Nay, thereto I shall not consent, nor any man shall I respite.
And yet swiftly and to the heart I shall spite, without any advisement.
And now, for a time, I will be high, but make thee sure that ye be ready shortly,
for thou mayst say that no living man may escape this day. Alas, I may well weep with sighs deep. Now have I no matter of company to help me in my journey and need to keep
And also my writing is full unready
How shall I do now for to excuse me?
I would to God I had never been God
To my soul a full great prophet it had be

The time has hit and is almost to go For what well would I work to do? To whom were I best my complaint to make? What, and I to fellowship thereof spake, and showed him of this sudden chance? For in him is all mine abeyance. We have in the world so many a day. Be good friends in sports and play. I see him yonder certainly. I trust that he will bear me company. Therefore to him will I speak to ease my sorrow. Well met, good fellowship, and good morning, man. Good morning by this day. But lookest thou so piteously. If anything be a mess, I pray thee be safe, so I may help to remedy. Yea, fellowship, yea, I am in great jeopardy. My true friend, show me to your mind. I would not forsafe each my life's end in the way of good company. That was well spoken, and lovingly. Sir, I must need to know your heaviness. I bid you to see any distress, if any have you wronged. And then she shall be, though I have the ground be slain through thee, and before that I know that I shall die. Barely fellowship, crammer see. Cush! By thy thanks I said not a straw. Show me your grief, and say no more. If I my heart should to you break, and you turn your mind from me, and not me comfort when ye hear me speak, then ten times sorrier I should be. Sure, I say, as I will do indeed. Then you be a good friend at need. I have found you true here before. and so ye shall evermore. For in faith, and thou go to hell, I would not forsake thee by the way. Ye speak like a good friend, I believe you well. I shall deserve it, and I may. I speak of no deserving by this day, for he that will say nothing new is unworthy of your company to go. Therefore, show me to the grief of your mind. As to your friend, most loving and kind, I shall show you how it is. Commanded I am by a messenger, that is, a high king's chief officer, to go a long journey, hard and dangerous, to give a reckoning strength. Wherefore, I bear you, bear me company as ye have promised in this journey. That is matter indeed. Promise is duty. But should I take to voyage upon thee, I know it well it should be to my pain. Also, it makes me appear ascertained, so let us take counsel while still we can, for your words would fear a strong man. Why, ye said if I had need, ye would never forsake me, quicknaked dead, though it were to hell truly. So I said, certainly, but such pleasure to be set aside the sooth to say. If we were to go on such a journey, when should we come again? Nay, never again, till the day of doom. In fact, I will not that way go. Who hath you these tidings brought? Indeed, death was with me here. By all that God hath bought, if death were the messenger, for no man living today would I set foot on that low journey. Not for the father that begat me. Yea, thereto yea, your mind will sooner apply To go to mirth soulless in play, Than to bear me company in my long journey. In faith I will not that way go, But if thou wouldst eat and drink and make good cheater, Or haunt to women the lusty company. I would not forsake thee while the day is clear. Trust me, verily. Oh, that is simple advice indeed. Now, gentle fellow, help me in my necessity. We have loved long, and now I need. Now, gentle fellowship, remember me. Whether ye have loved me or not, if I say John, I will not with not with thee go. Yes, I pray thee, take the labor and do so much for me. Fellowship, grant mercy. What, and thou would buy me a new gown? Nay, if thou had not carried, I would not have forsaken thee so. So now, as God speed thee on thy journey, I will depart from thee as fast as I may.
Whither away, Fellowship, will ye forsake me? Yea, by my faith to God I betake thee.
Farewell, good Fellowship, for this my heart is sore. At year forever I shall see thee no more.

Farewell, every man, now and then. For thee I shall remember that parting is mourning. Alack, shall we thus depart indeed? O lady, help without any more comfort. Lo, fellowship forsaketh me in my most need. His words were very pleasant and gay, and yet now he has gone away. Whither for secure shall I flee, if that fellowship hath forsaken me? To my kinsmen I will truly, praying them to help me in my necessity. I believe that they will do so, for kind will creep where it may not go. I go safe, for yonder I see them go. Where be ye now, my friends and kinsmen lo? You're viewing out your commanders. Cousin, my friend, show us your intent in any wise, and do not spare.
Cousin Everman, and to us declare, if ye be disposed to go any whither, for, want ye well, we will live and die together.
In wealth and woe we will to hold, for over his kin a man may be bold.

When you see my friends and kinsmen kind, Now shall I show you the grief of my heart. Commended I am by a messenger, That is, a high king's chief officer. He bade me go on pilgrimage to my pain, And I know full well I shall never come again. And I also must give a reckoning straight, for I have a great enemy half in weight, which intended for me to hinder. What account then that ye must render? That's what I know. Of all my works I must show, how I have lived and my days spent, and also of ill deeds that I have used in my time sin life was me lent and also of all virtues i have refused therefore i pray you to come with me to make my reckoning forcing to charity what to go with her is that the matter nay every man i have life for fast bread and this while here at home. Alas, that ever I was born. If you were saving me, how shall I ever be merrier class? Oh, watch, sir. He be a merry man. Take good heart, and make no wrong move. But one thing I warn you, by Satan, as for me, ye shall go alone. My cousin, will you not with me go?
No, Fire Lady, I have cramped in my toe.
Trust not to me, for so God you speed, I will deceive you in your most need.
If it faileth not us to play, ye shall have my maid with all my heart.
She loveth to go to feasts, there to be nice, and to dance, then abroad to start.

I will give her leave to join you in that journey, if that you and she may agree. No, show me the very effect of your mind. Will you not go with me, or abide behind? Abide behind. Yea, that will I, and I may. Therefore, farewell, till another day. alas
shall I never be married or glad
for fair
promises meant to be make gets when I have need they need forsake I am deceived that's making me sad Cousin every man, farewell now, for for merrily I will not go with you.
Also, of mine own life and unready reckoning I have to account, therefore I make care of him. Now God keep thee, for now I go. Ah, Jesus, it's all come here to?
Lo, fair words maketh fools vain.
They promise, and nothing will do, certaine.
My kinsmen promised me faith,
to abide by me steadfastly.

Yet fast away do they flee. Even so, fellowship promised me. Yet, in my mind a thing there is. All my life I have loved riches. If that my good would help me might, it would make my heart full light. I will speak to him in this distress. Where art thou, my goods and riches?

Who calleth me? Every man? What hast thou? I lie here in corners, trussed and piled so high, and in chests I have locked fast. Also sacked in bags thou mayest see with thine eye. I cannot serve in this past, though, where I lie. What will the hell likely be said? Come hither, good, in all the haste thou may, for of counsel I must desire thee. Sir, if ye in the world have sorrow or adversity, that I can help you to remedy shortly. It is another disease that grieves me. It is not of this world I tell thee so. I am sent for another way to go, to give a straight account general, before the highest Jupiter of all. And all my life I have loved thee. Therefore I pray thee, go with me, for peradventure thou mayest before God Almighty my reckoning to help clean and purify. For it is said, ever among that money maketh all right that is wrong. Nay, every man, I sing another song. I follow no man in such voyages, for, that I went with thee, thou shouldst fare much the worse for me, for, on me thou didst set thy mind, thy reckoning I have made plotter than thine. Alas, I should ever be afraid if I come to that fearful answer without thee.
Therefore, come hither, and let's go together.
Nay, I am too brittle. I am nothing to her. I follow Gabriela for the leading show.
Alas, I have thee loved all my life, and have had great pleasure in my goods and treasure.

That is to my damnation without line, my love. It is contrary to the love everlasting, but it now hath loved me moderately jerked, and to the bold given a part for the love of me, that would not in this delure have been, nor in this great sorrow and care. I was deceived, ere I was where, and I may weep misspending of my time. It it thou that I am vile? I had went, so. Nay, ever, I said no. As for a while, I was lengthy and a-season, Fountain heavy in prosperity. My condition is man-soaked to kill. If I stay far, how shall I do, Spil? Deem'st thou that I will follow thee? Nay, not from this world, verily, I had weaved otherwise.
Therefore to thy soul givest is a thief, for when thou art dead, this is my guise, another to deceive, and the same wise, as I have done thee, and of thy soul's grief.

O false good, cursed may thou be! Th thou traitor to God, thou hast deceived me, and caught me in thy snare. Mary, thou broughtest thyself in here, whereof I am very glad, I must be glad, I cannot be sad. O good, thou hast had long my hearty love, which should have been given to the Lord above.

But wilt thou not go with me? I pray truth to say. No, so God needs to be. Therefore, farewell, and have a good day. Oh, to whom will I best my moan to make, or to find company in that long journey? First, Fellowship. He said he would with me gone. His words were very pleasant and gay, but afterward he left me alone. Then spake I to my kinsmen, all in despair. They also gave me words fair. They lacked no fair speaking, but all forsake me in the ending. Then went I to my goods, who I loved best, in hopes to have found comfort, but there had I blessed. For my goods sharply did me tell that he bringeth many in hell And so was I ashamed So I am worthy to be blamed And so I may well myself hate Of whom counsel shall I now take I I think that I shall never speed.
Till that I go to my good deeds.
Although she is so weak that she can no longer go nor speak,
yet will I venture on her now.
My good deeds, where be you? Here I lie, cold in the ground, my sins having me so sore bound that I cannot stir.

Oh, good deeds, I stand in great fear. I must you do by me, that journey with you will I take. Therefore, I come to you, my moment to man. I pray thee, let's go thither together. I would full vain, but I cannot stand verily. Why? Is there anything on you flawed? Yea, sir, I may thank you of all. If ye had perfectly cured me, your book of account full ready now would be. Look, the books of your works and deeds are weak. Behold how they lie under the feet to your soul's heaviness. Our Lord Jesus, help me, for one letter herein can I not see. Here is a blind reckoning in time of distress. O good deeds, help me in this need, or else I am forever damned indeed. Help me to make my reckoning before the redeemer of all things, that he is and was and ever shall. Every man, I am sorry of your fall, and fain would I help you, and I were able. My good deeds, your counsel, I pray you, you give me that shall I do verily though that on my feet I may not go I have a sister that shall with you also call knowledge that shall with you abide to help you make that dreadful reckoning every man I will go with thee and be thy guide guide, in thy most need, to go by thy side. In good condition I am now in every thing. I thank God my creature. And when he hath brought thee there, for to heal thee of thy smart, go thou with thy reckoning and good deeds together, for to make thee joyful at the part before the blessed Trinity. Now go we together, lovingly, to confession, that cleansing river. Kneel down and ask mercy, for he is in good condition. Kneel down and ask mercy, for he is in good conceit with God Almighty.

Oh, blessed mountain head, that all uncleanness doth clarify Wash from me the spots of vices unclean, That on me no sin may be seen. O Shrent, mother of salvation, Redempt with heart and full contrition, Spare me now of my sins. I know her sore well every year, because with knowledge she comes to me.

I will eat her fruit as well as I can. And a precious jewel I will give thee, called penance, void of adversity. Therewith shall your body chastise thee, abstinence and perseverance in God's service. Herewith shall you receive that scourge of me, which is pennant and strong, he bested you.
Remember thy saviour, who scourged for thee, and sharp scourges, and suffered impatiently.
So must thou, ere thou pass thy poor departure.
Knowledge, keep him in this voyage, and by that time good deeds shall be with thee.
But in any wise, be sure of mercy.

Your time draws fast, but he will save it thee. Ask God mercy, and he will grant truth. When with the scourge of penance man doth him bind, the oil of forgiveness they shall be found. I'll pass it be, Jesus, all their speech. For now I am ready of penance. God be our guide.
Everyman,
look your penance that ye fulfill.
What pain that ever
it see you meet.
And I will give you counsel at will.
How your account
ye shall make clearly.
O eternal God,

O heavenly figure, a way of right wiseness O vision pure which descended down in a virgin pure because he would every man redeem which Adam forfeited in his disobedience O blessed Godhead elect and high, redeem me with my sins, that I may not go to purgatory. Knowledge, give me the scourge of penance. Every man, God give you time and space. Thus, I bequeath you in the hands of our Savior. Now may you make your reckoning sure.
What? I mean to this were an allegory.

In the name of all the Holy Trinity, my body punished, sore shall be. Take this body for the sin of the flesh, and also thou delightest to go gay and fresh. now my purgatory I will wait the water clear as to avoid that purgatory that sharp fire I thank God now I can walk and go and am healed of my sickness and woe therefore Therefore, with every man I will go, and not spare. His good works I will help him to declare. Now, every man, be merry and glad. Your good deeds cometh now. Ye may not be sad. Now is your good deeds whole and sound, going upright upon the ground. My heart is light, and shall be be evermore Now will I smite Faster than I did before Every man, pilgrim, my special friend Blessed be thou without end For thee has prepared the eternal glory Ye have made me whole and sound Therefore I will abide by thee in every sound Oh,, welcome, good deeds, for now I hear thy voice. Have we clear our reckoning? Yea, indeed, I have here. Excellent. Then, friends, I think we need not tarry. Be no more sad, but evermore rejoice. God seeth thy living in his throne above. Put on this garment, to thy behold, with which your tears is now well wet, lest before God it be unsweat when ye to your journey's end shall. what do ye call it is the garment of sorrow from pain it will you borrow
contrition it is that get its forgiveness it pleases God It is the garment of sorrow. From pain it will you borrow.
Contrition it is that get its forgiveness. It pleaseth God passing well.
Everyman, will you wear it for your health?
Yea, verily. Now, friends, I think we need not to depart in twain.
Nay, everyman, that we will not sustain. Yet must thou leave with thee three persons of great might. Who should they be? Strength and discretion, they hight, and your beauty may not abide you.
Ye must also call to mind your five wits, as for your counselors.
You must have them ready at all hours.
How shall I get them hither?
Ye must call them all together, and they will hear you in continent.

My friends, I call you all here to be present. Discretion, strength, my five wits and beauty. Here at your will you be all ready. What's the need that we should do? That we with every man would go. Advise me, will you go with him or not? He's not going in. We will bring him in there and talk of him, you may believe me. So will we go with him all together? Rather so, my friends. Now shall I give my final testament to all ye who hear our presence? In almost half my good I will give me, in the way of charity will go intent, and the other half still shall remain. I return to where it once went. I do so in despite of the fate of hell, and to avoid purgatory that sharp peril.
Every man, hearken what I will say. Go to the priesthood, you I advise, and receive of him, in any wise, the holy sacrament and ointment together, then shortly see ye turn again hither.

We shall all abide you here. Now, Jesu, be your altar speed, now, and lie true on the Holy Host. Now, friends, I, on pilgrimage, must continue to take. Come with me, thither I will go forward. Every time we will hunt from thee go, till we have gotten this voyage long. I, Discretion, will abideide by you also. And though this pilgrimage be never so strong, I will never part you fro. Every man I will be is sure by thee, as ever I was by Judas Maccabee. Alas, I am so faint I may not stand, my limbs me do fold let us not return back to this land not for all the world's gold for into this cave must I creep and turn to the earth and there to sleep alas into this grave? Yea, there shall ye consume, for and less. What, as I to wither here, yet, to remain and live on this earth, I may not, but before God Almighty in heaven above. I cross out all this, adieu, by St. I take my cap in my lap and I'm gone what beauty wither ye will forsake me peace I am deaf I look not behind me naught and thou wilt give me all the gold in thy chest oh to whom shall I trust beauty hath had me me high when she had promised to live with me and die. Every man I shall thee also forsake. This game liketh me not at all. What, strength will ye all forsake? Strength, just a little space, tarry. Nay, by the cross of grace I hide me from thee fast, though thou weep to thy hot breast. Ye promised otherwise. Ye be old enough, I see, your pilgrimage to take on hand. I repent thee, hither I came. You to displease, I am to blame. Yet promise is debt, need yet well want. As for that, I cannot. Thou art but a fool to complain. Thou spendest thy speech, and wastest thy brain. Go thrust thee into the ground. Both beauty and strength doth fly away fast, Who else but to God shall I now trust? Every man, I will after strength be gone, As for me, I will leave you alone. Whither will he, discretion, will he forsake me? Yea, in faith, for when strength is gone before, I follow after evermore. With the love of all the Holy Trinity, will you once look into my grave piteously? Yea, so nigh will I come. Now farewell, fellows, everyone. Alas, now discretion doth leave, Discretion, strength, beauty, They all promise to abide by me, Yet when death bloweth his blast, Fast away do they flee. Every man, none, Thee I forsake, I follow the other, And my leave I take. Yet I may well weep, for I considered you to be my best friend. I will keep thee near, but thee I mourn. Farewell forever, nevermore. Now fellowship hath forsaken me. There is no one else I can trust, save perhaps my good deeds.
Will ye forsake me?
Nay, every man, I will abide by thee.
I shall not forsake thee indeed.
Thou shalt find me a good friend at least.
Gramercy, good deeds, and knowledge, will ye forsake me?

Yea, when ye to your death shall come, but not yet, for no manner of danger. Grant me some knowledge with all my heart. Nay, yet I shall not from hence depart, till I see where ye shall be come. Is it time to leave good deeds, to depart? Yea, every man. All earthly things is but vanity. Beauty, strength, and discretion do man forsake. Foolish friends and kinsmen that fair spake. All flee it to say good deeds, and that am I. Ye think, alas, that I must be gone. Hear all ye who what and see, for I did forsake my good deeds when I trust of having her. I believe now I shall. Fear not, I shall speak for thee.
Have mercy on me, God Almighty, and mother named Mary.
Short our ends and finish our pain. Let us go, and never come again.
Here I cry thee, mercy! In thy hands, my soul, I commend.
Receive it, Lord, that it be not lost.
To save me from the fiend's boast.
That I may be with
that blessed host
that shall be saved
the day of doom.
In manus tuas
of might most.
Forever
commendo spiritum
Thank you. In manus tuas, of might most, forever commendo spiritum, ma'am.

Now hath he suffered that which we all shall endure, The good deeds shall make all sure. Now hath he made ending, methinketh I hear angels sing, And make great joy and melody, where every man's soul shall receive to be. Now come, excellent, elect spouse to Jesu, your boss thou shalt build, now that thy soul has taken thy body from, thy reckoning is crystal clear, and thou shalt enter the heavenly sphere, unto which all ye that liveth well shalt go after the day of doom. This memory may all men have in mind, ye hearers, take it of worth, old and young. And for safe pride, for he deceiveth you in the end. And remember, beauty, five wits, strength, and discretion. They all at last do every man forsake. Save his good deeds, them there he doth take.
But beware for, and may be small,
before God he hath no help at all.
There too help the Trinity.
Amen, say ye, forsake charity. Sadie, for St. Cherry. Thank you. Applaus © BF-WATCH TV 2021
